text: The robotic surgical system is docked, and the surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery at the pelvic brim, taking care to avoid the hypogastric nerves. The artery is isolated cleanly two centimeters from its origin at the abdominal aorta. Hem-o-lok clips are applied for ligation, and the artery is divided using monopolar scissors.

The robotic surgical system is docked, and the surgeon isolates the femur's enteric artery at the pelvic brim, taking care to avoid damage to the hypogastric nerves. The eye tree is isolated cleanly, two centimeters from its origin at the abdominal aorta.

The surgeon uses Hem-o-lock clips to ligate the isolated femoral enteric artery at the pelvic brim, followed by division with monopolar scissors. Dissection proceeds cranially using a medial collateral approach, employing traction and counter-traction in front of Gerota's fascia. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated at the lower border of the pancreas, secured with locking clips, and divided with monopolar scissors, allowing precise dissection laterally, cranially, and caudally.

The surgeon secures the artery and proceeds with cranial dissection using a medial collateral approach, applying traction and counter-traction in front of the Gerota's fascia. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated at its origin near the lower border of the pancreas, secured with locking clips, and divided using a monopolar scissor. The dissection continues laterally, cranially, and caudally, minimizing trauma to the left ureter and gonadal vessels by careful traction with the robotic left hand.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to perform dissection in front of the rotous fascia, minimizing trauma to the left ureter and glidal vessel. Traction is carefully applied with the robotic left arm to avoid tissue damage due to lack of tactile feedback. The dissection proceeds behind the left colon and at the top of the pancreas, followed by lateral mobilization of the left colon to achieve complete mobilization of the splenic flexure. The surgeon then focuses on rectal dissection, starting posteriorly between the hypogastric nerves using low voltage monopolar diathermy, with gentle traction applied using the robotic left arm and angulated instruments.

The surgeon completes the dissection behind the left colon and at the top of the pancreas, then shifts focus to the lateral mobilization of the left colon to achieve complete mobilization of the splenic flexure. Traction and counter-traction are applied using both robotic arms. Following this, attention is directed to rectal dissection, starting posteriorly between the two hypogastric nerves using low voltage monopolar diathermy. The robotic left arm and an angulated instrument are used to apply gentle traction, facilitating the dissection while avoiding damage to the hypogastric nerves.

The robotic surgery setup provides a stable camera view as the surgeon uses an angulated instrument to continue posterior dissection at the top of the boulder fascia. The rectal tube remains lightly attached on the left side to minimize specimen rotation. The focus shifts to the right side of the peritoneal flexion rectum, with careful attention to avoid damaging the right epigastric nerve, which is closely attached to the mesorectal package.

The stable camera view shows the robotic angulated instrument facilitating posterior dissection at the top of Waldeyer's fascia. The surgeon focuses on right rectal mobilization, leaving the rectal tube lightly attached on the left side to prevent specimen rotation. The right epigastric nerve is visible near the mesorectal package, requiring careful navigation to avoid damage.

The surgeon continues mobilizing the rectal dissection on the right posterolateral side, ensuring careful avoidance of the left hypogastric nerve. The dissection progresses past Valdai's fascia using previously described techniques. Attention shifts to performing an N-block hysterectomy, starting with the isolation of the round ligaments and mobilizing the uterus from the back of the bladder to access the vasculature.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectal dissection further on the right posterolateral side, ensuring to avoid injury to the left hypogastric nerve. The dissection progresses past Valdai's fascia. The focus shifts to an en bloc hysterectomy for an anteriorly placed tumor tethering the back wall of the uterus. The round ligaments are isolated first, and the uterus is mobilized from the back of the bladder to access the vasculature. A bipolar energy device in the left robotic hand seals the vasculature, while the right hand uses a combination of bipolar energy and monopolar scissors to divide the tibiovarian vasculature.

The surgeon uses a bipolar energy device in the left robotic hand to ensure complete vascular sealing, creating a bloodless plane. The right vasculature is sealed using bipolar energy, followed by division of the tibiovarian vasculature with a combination of bipolar energy and monopolar scissors in the right robotic hand. This exposes the right anterolateral aspect of the uterus. Similar steps are performed on the left side, using bipolar energy and monopolar scissors to maintain a hemostatic operative field. Both uterine arteries are isolated and divided close to the specimen, safeguarding the ureters on both sides.

The surgeon exposes the right anterolateral aspect of the uterus, using bipolar energy and monopolar scissors to maintain a hemostatic field while isolating and dividing uterine vessels close to the specimen. A swab on a stick is introduced through the vagina to highlight the cervix, facilitating revision of the cervical wall with monopolar diathermy. The rectal specimen is mobilized anterolaterally on both sides, avoiding damage to the pelvic hypogastric nerves. The rectal tube is divided using an endo-GIA stapler, and the defect in the posterior wall of the vagina and cervix is closed with a running hemo-lock suture, showcasing precise suturing with robotic instrumentation.

A swab on a stick is introduced through the vagina to highlight the back of the cervix, facilitating direct revision of the cervical wall using monoclonal diathermy. The rectal specimen is mobilized anterolaterally on both sides, with care taken to avoid excessive traction on the left pelvic hypogastric nerves. The vector tube is divided using an endo-GIA stapler, and the defect in the posterior wall of the vagina and cervix is closed with a running hemo-lock suture. The secure suture line is visible, and robotic instrumentation aids in precise suturing with minimal arm clashes.
After the rectal division, the surgeon extracts the specimen and performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapler, ensuring the continuity of the bowel.
